sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 12, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Maze navigation puzzle. Blue square with black dot (top-left of maze) is the player. Blue square with blue dot (bottom-right) is likely the goal. Red bar is an obstacle or moving element. White corridors are the path. Black areas are walls.
- Goal model: Navigate from the starting blue square to the target blue square through white corridors.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player through the maze.
- Open questions: How to reach the goal room which appears isolated by white corridors? Is the red bar interactive? What does SPACE do?
- Plan: Check if SPACE interacts with the red bar or if there's a hidden path. Let me first map the full maze layout more carefully.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 11 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have a clear compressed view of the maze. Let me analyze the black floor layout:
Row 7: B31 (cols 13-43)
Row 8: B b5 B25 W7 → player at cols 14-18, black at 13 and 19-43
Row 9: B b5 B20 R3 B2 W7 → red at cols 38-40, black at 39-40... wait let me decode more carefully
Actually, the compressed format is: char + count (if count > 1). Let me decode row by row:
Row 7: B31 W7 → B(13-43) W(44-50)
Row 8: B b5 B25 W7 → B(13) b(14-18) B(19-43) W(44-50) [player at 14-18]
Row 9: B b5 B20 R3 B2 W7 → B(13) b(14-18) B(19-38) R(39-41) B(42-43) W(44-50)
Row 10: B b2 B b2 B20 R3 B2 W7 → B(13) b(14-15) B(16) b(17-18) B(19-38) R(39-41) B(42-43) W(44-50)
Row 11: B b5 B20 R3 B2 W7 → B(13) b(14-18) B(19-38) R(39-41) B(42-43) W(44-50)
Row 12: B b5 B21 R B3 W7 → B(13) b(14-18) B(19-39) R(40) B(41-43) W(44-50)
Row 13: B27 R B3 W7 → B(13-39) R(40) B(41-43) W(44-50)
So the top area (rows 7-13) has black at cols 13-39 (or 13-43), with red at col 39-41. The player starts at cols 14-18.
Row 14-18: B7 W5 B7 W7 B R B W9 → B(13-19) W(20-24) B(25-31) W(32-38) B(39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 19-25: B19 W7 B R B W9 → B(13-31) W(32-38) B(39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 26-37: B7 W19 B R B W9 → B(13-19) W(20-38) B(39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 38: B R5 B W19 B R B W9 → B(13) R(14-18) B(19) W(20-38) B(39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 39: B2 R4 B21 R B W9 → B(13-14) R(15-18) B(19-39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 40: B R27 B W9 → B(13) R(14-40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 41: B2 R4 B23 W9 → B(13-14) R(15-18) B(19-41) W(42-50)
Row 42: B R5 B W31 → B(13) R(14-18) B(19) W(20-50)
Row 43-47: B7 W31 → B(13-19) W(20-50)
Row 48: B7 W22 B9 → B(13-19) W(20-41) B(42-50)
Row 49: B30 b7 B → B(13-42) b(43-49) B(50)
Row 50-51: B36 b B → B(13-48) b(49) B(50)
Row 52: B33 b B2 b B → B(13-45) b(46) B(47-48) b(49) B(50)
Row 53-54: B36 b B → B(13-48) b(49) B(50)
Row 55: B30 b7 B → B(13-42) b(43-49) B(50)
Row 56: W29 B9 → W(13-41) B(42-50)
Now I see the full picture! The key observations:
1. The red bar at rows 38-42 forms a cross:
   - Horizontal: rows 38-42, cols 14-18
   - Vertical: col 40, rows 8-40
   - Row 40: red spans cols 14-40 (full horizontal bar)
2. The black floor has these connected components:
   - Top area: rows 7-13, cols 13-43 (with some red at cols 39-41)
   - Left corridor: rows 14-37, cols 13-19
   - Middle gap: rows 14-18, cols 25-31
   - Middle area: rows 19-25, cols 13-31
   - Right corridor: rows 14-37, cols 39, 41 (too narrow for 5-wide player)
   - Below red bar: rows 39-41, cols 19-39/41 (but row 40 has red at cols 14-40)
   - Bottom left: rows 42-47, cols 13-19
   - Goal room: rows 48-55, cols 13-50 (with goal at cols 43-49)
3. The red bar at row 40 spans cols 14-40, completely blocking horizontal passage.
4. The bottom area (rows 42-55) has black at cols 13-19 (rows 42-47) and cols 13-50 (rows 48-55).
Wait, row 48: B(13-19) W(20-41) B(42-50). So the black at cols 13-19 is separated from cols 42-50 by white at cols 20-41. But row 49: B(13-42) b(43-49) B(50). So the black at cols 13-42 connects to the goal at cols 43-49.
So the path to the goal is:
1. Reach rows 48-52, cols 13-19 (bottom left)
2. Move DOWN to rows 48-52 (already there)
3. Move RIGHT to cols 19-23... but row 48 has W(20-41), so the player at rows 48-52, cols 19-23 would overlap with white at row 48, cols 20-23.
Wait, but the player is 5 rows tall. If the player is at rows 48-52, cols 13-19:
- Row 48, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 49, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 50, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 51, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 52, cols 13-19: B ✓
To move RIGHT, the player would go to rows 48-52, cols 19-23:
- Row 48, cols 19-23: B(19) W(20-23) → white at cols 20-23, BLOCKED!
So the player can't move RIGHT from cols 13-19 at rows 48-52 because row 48 has white at cols 20-41.
But what if the player is at rows 49-53, cols 13-19? Then:
- Row 49, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 50, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 51, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 52, cols 13-19: B ✓
- Row 53, cols 13-19: B ✓
To move RIGHT, the player would go to rows 49-53, cols 19-23:
- Row 49, cols 19-23: B ✓
- Row 50, cols 19-23: B ✓
- Row 51, cols 19-23: B ✓
- Row 52, cols 19-23: B ✓
- Row 53, cols 19-23: B ✓
So if the player is at rows 49-53, it CAN move RIGHT! But how to reach rows 49-53 from the left corridor?
The left corridor is at rows 14-37, cols 13-19. Below that:
- Row 38: B(13) R(14-18) B(19) → red at cols 14-18 blocks the player
- Rows 39-41: red blocks
- Row 42: B(13) R(14-18) B(19) → red at cols 14-18 blocks
- Rows 43-47: B(13-19) → black, but how to reach from above?
The red bar at rows 38-42, cols 14-18 blocks the left corridor. The player can't pass through.
So the only way to reach the bottom area (rows 42-55) is... somehow bypassing the red bar.
Wait, let me look at rows 39-41 more carefully:
- Row 39: B(13-14) R(15-18) B(19-39) R(40) B(41)
- Row 40: B(13) R(14-40) B(41)
- Row 41: B(13-14) R(15-18) B(19-41)
The black area at rows 39-41, cols 19-39/41 is isolated from the top by the white corridor at rows 26-37, cols 20-38. And it's isolated from the left by the red bar at cols 14-18.
But wait! Row 39 has B(19-39), which is 21 columns wide. And row 41 has B(19-41), which is 23 columns wide. If the player can reach this area, it can move RIGHT to cols 35-39, then DOWN to rows 44-48... but rows 42-47 have W(20-50), so the player can't move DOWN from rows 39-41.
Hmm, I'm really stuck. The red bar completely blocks all paths from the top to the bottom.
Wait, let me reconsider. What if the player CAN move through red cells? Earlier I thought the 5th DOWN was blocked, but maybe it was blocked for a different reason (e.g., the player would overlap with white cells).
Let me check: the player at [32,14]-[36,18] tries to move DOWN to [38,14]-[42,18].
- Row 38, cols 14-18: R R R R R
- Row 39, cols 14-18: B R R R R
- Row 40, cols 14-18: R R R R R
- Row 41, cols 14-18: B R R R R
- Row 42, cols 14-18: R R R R R
So the player would overlap with red cells. If red is a wall, the move is blocked. But what if red is NOT a wall?
Actually, I realize I never actually tested whether red is a wall or not. The 5th DOWN was blocked, but I assumed it was because of red. Let me verify by checking what happened to the player position.
Actually, I already know the player is at [32,14]-[36,18] after 5 DOWNs, which means only 4 DOWNs were effective (from [8,14] to [32,14] is 4 * 6 = 24 rows). So the 5th DOWN was indeed blocked.
But wait, maybe the 5th DOWN was blocked because the player would overlap with white cells, not red cells. Let me check:
- Player at [32,14]-[36,18], move DOWN to [38,14]-[42,18]
- Row 38, cols 14-18: R (not white)
- Row 39, cols 14-18: B R R R R (not all white)
- Row 40, cols 14-18: R (not white)
- Row 41, cols 14-18: B R R R R (not all white)
- Row 42, cols 14-18: R (not white)
So the player would overlap with red and black cells, not white. If the move was blocked, it must be because red is a wall.
OK so red IS a wall. The question is: how to reach the goal?
Let me think about this differently. What if the goal is NOT to reach the blue square at the bottom right? What if the goal is to do something else with the red bar?
Looking at the maze, the red bar forms a "T" or "cross" shape. What if the player needs to "touch" or "activate" the red bar? Or what if SPACE near the red bar does something?
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. The player leaves a gray trail. What if the goal is to fill the ENTIRE black area with gray? The human solution is ~78 actions, which is a lot. If the goal is to visit every black cell, that would require many actions.
Let me count the black cells:
- Top area: rows 7-13, cols 13-43 ≈ 7 * 31 = 217 cells
- Left corridor: rows 14-37, cols 13-19 ≈ 24 * 7 = 168 cells
- Middle gap: rows 14-18, cols 25-31 ≈ 5 * 7 = 35 cells
- Middle area: rows 19-25, cols 13-31 ≈ 7 * 19 = 133 cells
- Right corridor: rows 14-37, cols 39, 41 ≈ 24 * 2 = 48 cells (but too narrow for player)
- Bottom area: rows 42-55, cols 13-50 ≈ 14 * 38 = 532 cells
Total ≈ 1133 cells. The player is 5x5 = 25 cells, so it would take about 1133/25 ≈ 45 actions to cover all cells. But the human solution is ~78 actions, which is more.
Hmm, but the player can't reach the bottom area because of the red bar. So maybe the goal is not to visit all cells.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if the red bar is a "bridge" that the player can cross by pressing SPACE? Or what if the red bar moves when the player presses a certain key?
Let me try pressing SPACE when the player is near the red bar to see if it does something.
Actually, wait. I just realized something. The player is at [8,14]-[12,18] after SPACE reset. Let me try moving RIGHT to [8,20]-[12,24], then RIGHT to [8,26]-[12,30], then DOWN to [14,26]-[18,30], then DOWN to [20,26]-[24,30], then LEFT to [20,20]-[24,24], then LEFT to [20,14]-[24,18], then DOWN to [26,14]-[30,18], then DOWN to [32,14]-[36,18], then... stuck at red bar.
But what if I go RIGHT from [20,26]-[24,30] instead of LEFT? Let me check:
- [20,26]-[24,30], move RIGHT to [20,32]-[24,36]
- Row 20, cols 32-36: W W W W W (white at cols 32-38)
- So the player can't move RIGHT from [20,26]-[24,30].
OK so from the middle area, the player can only go LEFT or DOWN. Going DOWN leads to the left corridor (rows 26-37, cols 13-19), which is blocked by red. Going LEFT leads to the left corridor (rows 19-25, cols 13-19), which also eventually leads to the red bar.
I'm completely stuck. Let me try something different: what if I move RIGHT from the start position all the way to the right edge of the black area, then DOWN?
Start: [8,14]-[12,18]
RIGHT: [8,20]-[12,24]
RIGHT: [8,26]-[12,30]
RIGHT: [8,32]-[12,36]
- Row 8, cols 32-36: B B B B B ✓
- Row 9, cols 32-36: B B B B B ✓
- Row 10, cols 32-36: B B B B B ✓
- Row 11, cols 32-36: B B B B B ✓
- Row 12, cols 32-36: B B B B B ✓
So this works!
RIGHT: [8,38]-[12,42]
- Row 8, cols 38-42: B B B B B ✓ (row 8: B(19-43))
- Row 9, cols 38-42: B R R B B → red at cols 39-41, BLOCKED!
So the player can reach [8,32]-[12,36] but not [8,38]-[12,42] because of red at cols 39-41.
From [8,32]-[12,36], can I move DOWN?
- DOWN: [14,32]-[18,36]
- Row 14, cols 32-36: W W W W W (white at cols 32-38), BLOCKED!
So from [8,32]-[12,36], the player can't move DOWN (white) or RIGHT (red). The only option is LEFT (back).
Hmm, so the right side of the top area is also blocked. The only path from the top area is through the middle gap at cols 25-31.
OK let me think about this more carefully. The maze has:
1. Top area (rows 7-13, cols 13-43)
2. Middle gap (rows 14-18, cols 25-31)
3. Middle area (rows 19-25, cols 13-31)
4. Left corridor (rows 26-37, cols 13-19)
5. Right corridor (rows 14-37, cols 39, 41) - too narrow
6. Red bar (rows 38-42)
7. Bottom area (rows 42-55)
The player can navigate from 1 → 2 → 3 → 4, but 4 is blocked by 6. And 5 is too narrow.
So the player can't reach the bottom area. Unless... the red bar is not a permanent obstacle.
Let me try pressing SPACE when the player is near the red bar to see if it does something special.
Actually, I just realized I should check if there's a path I missed. Let me look at the right corridor more carefully.
Rows 14-37: B(39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
So the right corridor has black at cols 39 and 41, with red at col 40. The player is 5 columns wide, so it can't fit through.
But wait, what if the player can partially overlap with the red bar? Like, the player at cols 38-42 would have:
- Col 38: W (white)
- Col 39: B (black)
- Col 40: R (red)
- Col 41: B (black)
- Col 42: W (white)
So the player would overlap with white at cols 38 and 42, which are walls. So the player can't be at cols 38-42.
What about cols 37-41?
- Col 37: W (white, rows 14-37)
- Col 38: W (white)
- Col 39: B (black)
- Col 40: R (red)
- Col 41: B (black)
Again, white at cols 37-38 blocks the player.
So the right corridor is indeed too narrow. The player can't pass through.
I'm really stuck. Let me try pressing SPACE at different locations to see if it does something special.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. What if the goal is NOT to reach the blue square at the bottom right? What if the goal is to fill the maze with gray (visit all black cells)?
If the goal is to visit all black cells, then the player needs to navigate through the top area, middle gap, middle area, and left corridor. The bottom area is unreachable, so maybe the goal is just to visit all reachable black cells.
But the human solution is ~78 actions, which seems like a lot for just visiting the reachable area. Let me estimate:
- Top area: rows 7-13, cols 13-43 ≈ 217 cells
- Middle gap: rows 14-18, cols 25-31 ≈ 35 cells
- Middle area: rows 19-25, cols 13-31 ≈ 133 cells
- Left corridor: rows 14-37, cols 13-19 ≈ 168 cells
Total ≈ 553 cells. Player covers 25 cells per action (5x5), but there's overlap. So maybe ~30-40 actions to cover all reachable cells.
78 actions seems like a lot, but maybe the player needs to backtrack a lot to cover all cells.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the goal. What if the goal IS to reach the blue square, and there's a way to bypass the red bar that I'm missing?
Let me look at the red bar again:
Row 38: B(13) R(14-18) B(19) W(20-38) B(39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 39: B(13-14) R(15-18) B(19-39) R(40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 40: B(13) R(14-40) B(41) W(42-50)
Row 41: B(13-14) R(15-18) B(19-41) W(42-50)
Row 42: B(13) R(14-18) B(19) W(20-50)
The black area at rows 39-41, cols 19-39/41 is isolated. But what if the player can reach it from the right side?
Looking at row 39: B(19-39) R(40) B(41). The black at cols 19-39 is separated from col 41 by red at col 40. So the player can't reach col 41 from cols 19-39.
But what about from above? Row 38: B(19) W(20-38) B(39). The black at col 19 is separated from col 39 by white at cols 20-38. So the player can't reach col 39 from col 19 at row 38.
And from below? Row 42: B(19) W(20-50). The black at col 19 is isolated.
So the black area at rows 39-41, cols 19-39/41 is completely isolated. The player can't reach it from any direction.
Wait, but row 39 has B(19-39), which is 21 columns wide. And the player is 5 columns wide. So if the player could reach this area, it could move around. But how to reach it?
The only way to reach rows 39-41 is from above (rows 34-38) or below (rows 42-46). From above, the white corridor at rows 26-37, cols 20-38 blocks access to cols 20-38. And the left corridor at cols 13-19 is blocked by red at rows 38-42. From below, rows 42-47 have white at cols 20-50, so the player can't reach rows 39-41 from below.
So the black area at rows 39-41, cols 19-39/41 is indeed unreachable.
OK, I think the goal might be to visit all reachable black cells (fill the maze with gray). Let me try to plan a path that covers all reachable black cells.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the bottom area. Row 49: B(13-42) b(43-49) B(50). The black at cols 13-42 connects to the goal at cols 43-49. And row 48: B(13-19) W(20-41) B(42-50). The black at cols 13-19 is separated from cols 42-50 by white at cols 20-41.
But row 49 has B(13-42), which connects cols 13-42. So if the player is at rows 49-53, cols 13-19, it can move RIGHT to cols 19-23, then further RIGHT to reach the goal.
The question is: how to reach rows 49-53, cols 13-19?
From the left corridor (rows 14-37, cols 13-19), the player can move DOWN to rows 32-36, cols 13-19. But the red bar at rows 38-42, cols 14-18 blocks further DOWN movement.
From below (rows 42-47, cols 13-19), the player can move UP to rows 37-41, cols 13-19. But row 38 has red at cols 14-18, which blocks the player.
So the bottom area (rows 42-55) is isolated from the top area by the red bar.
Unless... the red bar can be destroyed or moved. Let me try pressing SPACE when the player is near the red bar.
Actually, I just had another idea. What if the player needs to "paint" the red bar with gray (by moving over it)? But we already established that red is a wall, so the player can't move over it.
Or what if SPACE toggles the red bar (turns it into black or white)? Let me try pressing SPACE when the player is adjacent to the red bar.
Let me plan: move the player to [32,14]-[36,18] (just above the red bar), then press SPACE to see if it does something.
Actually, I realize I've been spending too many actions exploring. Let me try a more systematic approach. Let me write a BFS/pathfinding algorithm to find the shortest path from the start to the goal, considering the player's 5x5 size and the maze layout.
But first, I need to confirm whether the goal is reachable. If the red bar is an impassable wall, then the goal is unreachable, and I need to reconsider the puzzle mechanics.
Let me try one more thing: what if the player CAN move through red cells, but the 5th DOWN was blocked for a different reason?
Actually, I just realized something. The player at [32,14]-[36,18] tries to move DOWN to [38,14]-[42,18]. But wait, the player moves 6 cells per action. So from row 32, moving DOWN would go to row 38. But the player is 5 rows tall, so it occupies rows 38-42.
Row 38, cols 14-18: R R R R R
Row 39, cols 14-18: B R R R R
Row 40, cols 14-18: R R R R R
Row 41, cols 14-18: B R R R R
Row 42, cols 14-18: R R R R R
So the player would overlap with red cells at rows 38, 40, 42 and black cells at rows 39, 41. If red is a wall, the move is blocked.
But what if the player can move through red cells? Let me test this by trying to move RIGHT from the start position towards the red bar at cols 39-41.
Start: [8,14]-[12,18]
RIGHT: [8,20]-[12,24]
RIGHT: [8,26]-[12,30]
RIGHT: [8,32]-[12,36]
RIGHT: [8,38]-[12,42]
- Row 8, cols 38-42: B B B B B (row 8: B(19-43))
- Row 9, cols 38-42: B R R B B (row 9: B(19-38) R(39-41) B(42-43))
- Row 10, cols 38-42: B R R B B
- Row 11, cols 38-42: B R R B B
- Row 12, cols 38-42: B B R B B (row 12: B(19-39) R(40) B(41-43))
So the player at [8,38]-[12,42] would overlap with red at rows 9-12, cols 39-41. If red is a wall, this move is blocked.
Let me try this sequence and see if the 4th RIGHT is blocked.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: The red bar at rows 38-42 completely blocks the left corridor. The goal room at rows 49-55 seems unreachable via normal paths. Need to test if the player can move through red cells or if SPACE interacts with the red bar.
Plan: Test if player can move through red cells by trying RIGHT from start towards the red bar at cols 39-41. If blocked, try SPACE near the red bar.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test if player can move through red cells
# From start [8,14]-[12,18], move RIGHT 4 times
# RIGHT 1: [8,20]-[12,24] - all black, should work
# RIGHT 2: [8,26]-[12,30] - all black, should work
# RIGHT 3: [8,32]-[12,36] - all black, should work
# RIGHT 4: [8,38]-[12,42] - overlaps with red at rows 9-12, cols 39-41

action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 12, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Maze navigation puzzle. Blue square with black dot (top-left of maze) is the player. Blue square with blue dot (bottom-right) is likely the goal. Red bar is an obstacle or moving element. White corridors are the path. Black areas are walls.
- Goal model: Navigate from the starting blue square to the target blue square through white corridors.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player through the maze.
- Open questions: How to reach the goal room which appears isolated by white corridors? Is the red bar interactive? What does SPACE do?
- Plan: Check if SPACE interacts with the red bar or if there's a hidden path. Let me first map the full maze layout more carefully.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 11 of about 78 target actions so far.
